sections.markdown:
# Quasiquote, Unquote, Splicing

Writing `(list 'if test (list 'progn ...))` to build code is painful. The reader gives us three pieces of syntactic sugar to make it ergonomic:

* `` ` `` (backtick) — **quasiquote**. Like `'`, it returns a literal structure...
* `,` (comma) — ...except inside the quasiquote, an unquoted expression is **evaluated** and its value spliced in.
* `,@` (comma-at) — like `,`, but the value (which must be a list) is **spliced** into the surrounding list, not nested as a single element.

```lisp
(let ((x 10) (xs '(a b c)))
  `(start ,x middle ,@xs end))
;; => (START 10 MIDDLE A B C END)
```

Without `,@`, `,xs` would have produced `(START 10 MIDDLE (A B C) END)`.

Quasiquote is just sugar — the reader translates a backtick form into calls to `list`, `cons`, `append`, and `quote`. Different implementations may print it differently when you re-read it, so don't rely on a specific expansion shape.

This is the syntax we'll use to assemble macro expansions.

sections.explain: Comma evaluates X (10) and inserts its value as one element.
Plain comma inserts the LIST as a single element.
Comma-at SPLICES the list into the surrounding form.
Building a function-call form. We could EVAL this to get 7.
Comma can wrap any expression, including nested calls.

sections.prompt: Define WRAP-PAIR that takes a value X and returns the data form (WRAPPED X). Use quasiquote.
Define MAKE-CALL-FORM that takes an OP symbol and a list of ARGS and returns the call form (OP a1 a2 ...). Use quasiquote with comma-at.
